stem:
A custom print shop charges a one-time design fee plus a fixed cost per shirt. The table below shows the total cost $C$, in dollars, for ordering $n$ shirts.

| $n$ (shirts) | $C$ (dollars) |
|---|---|
| 2 | 30 |
| 5 | 45 |

Assuming $C$ is a linear function of $n$, what is the maximum number of shirts a school club can order if its budget is $95?
explanation: Step 1: Find the per-shirt cost (slope) using the two table values: $$m = \frac{45 - 30}{5 - 2} = \frac{15}{3} = 5 \text{ dollars per shirt}$$ Step 2: Find the one-time design fee (the $C$-intercept $b$) using the point $(2, 30)$: $$30 = 5(2) + b \implies 30 = 10 + b \implies b = 20$$ So $C(n) = 5n + 20$. Step 3: Find the maximum $n$ within the $95 budget: $$5n + 20 \leq 95 \implies 5n \leq 75 \implies n \leq 15$$ The maximum number of shirts the club can order is $\mathbf{15}$.

distractorRationale.A: Uses the per-order cost at $n = 2$ as a per-shirt rate ($30 \div 2 = 15$ per shirt) and ignores the design fee: solves $15n = 95$, giving $n \approx 6.3$, which rounds down to 6.
distractorRationale.B: Correctly finds the slope of 5 but uses the first table value as the $C$-intercept ($b = 30$ instead of computing $b = 20$): solves $5n + 30 = 95$, giving $5n = 65$, so $n = 13$.
distractorRationale.D: Correctly finds the slope of 5 but ignores the design fee entirely ($b = 0$): solves $5n = 95$, giving $n = 19$.
